title: Twitter / X Feed — System Design
description: Design a home-timeline system for a microblogging service: post a short message and have it appear in the feeds of millions of followers within seconds. The central tension is fanout-on-write vs fanout-on-read and how to reconcile them for celebrity accounts.

nodes.label: Mobile / Web Client
nodes.description: User's app; posts tweets and reads the home timeline.

nodes.label: Load Balancer
nodes.description: Terminates TLS and spreads requests across API servers.

nodes.label: Timeline API
nodes.description: Request-time service for posting tweets and reading timelines.

nodes.label: Tweet Write Service
nodes.description: Validates and persists a new tweet, then triggers fanout.

nodes.label: Timeline Read Service
nodes.description: Assembles a user's home timeline on read.

nodes.label: Fanout Service
nodes.description: Pushes a new tweet into each follower's timeline cache.

nodes.label: Fanout Queue
nodes.description: Buffers fanout work so posting stays fast and bursts absorb.

nodes.label: Social Graph Service
nodes.description: Resolves follower/following edges for fanout and reads.

nodes.label: Graph Store
nodes.description: Authoritative follower/following adjacency, sharded by user.

nodes.label: Tweet Store
nodes.description: Authoritative tweet records, sharded by tweet/author id.

nodes.label: Timeline Cache
nodes.description: Per-user precomputed home timeline (list of tweet ids).

nodes.label: Tweet Cache
nodes.description: Hot tweet contents by id, hydrated into timelines on read.

nodes.label: Media CDN
nodes.description: Serves images/video attached to tweets near the user.

nodes.label: Media Object Store
nodes.description: Durable blob storage for uploaded photos and video.

nodes.label: Ranking Service
nodes.description: Scores and reorders timeline candidates by relevance.

nodes.label: Search Index
nodes.description: Inverted index for tweet text and hashtag lookup.

functional: Post a tweet (text + optional media), visible to followers.
Read a home timeline: recent tweets from accounts you follow, newest-first or ranked.
Follow and unfollow accounts; the timeline reflects the current follow set.
View a single user's profile timeline (their own tweets).
Search tweets by keyword and hashtag (secondary).
nonFunctional: Read-heavy: home-timeline reads dominate writes by ~50-100:1.
Timeline read latency p99 < 200 ms — reads must be precomputed, not assembled from scratch.
A posted tweet should reach followers within a few seconds (eventual consistency is acceptable).
Highly available for reads; a brief fanout delay is tolerable, a read outage is not.
Scale to hundreds of millions of users and accounts with tens of millions of followers.

capacity.label: Tweets per second
capacity.note: Spikes far higher on major events.

capacity.label: Avg followers
capacity.note: Median is far lower; distribution is heavy-tailed.

capacity.label: Max followers (celebrity)
capacity.note: A single post can mean 100M timeline writes — the fanout problem.

capacity.label: Fanout writes/sec (peak)
capacity.note: 5K tweets/s x avg fanout, bursty around big accounts.

api.description: Post a new tweet. Returns immediately after persisting; fanout is async.
Read the authenticated user's home timeline (cursor-paginated).
Follow an account. Updates the social graph; future tweets fan out to this follower.
Search tweets by keyword or hashtag.

dataModel.name: tweets
dataModel.note: Authoritative tweet records, sharded by author or tweet id.

dataModel.name: follows
dataModel.note: Directed social graph. Two indexes: by follower and by followee.

dataModel.name: home_timeline
dataModel.note: Derived per-user list of tweet ids (in Timeline Cache, e.g. a capped Redis list).

dataModel.name: users
dataModel.note: Profile + counters; is_celebrity flag drives the hybrid read path.

patterns.name: Fanout-on-write
patterns.what: Precompute each follower's timeline at post time by pushing the tweet id into their cached list.
patterns.whenToUse: Read-heavy systems where reads must be cheap and writers have manageable fanout.

patterns.name: Fanout-on-read
patterns.what: Assemble the timeline at read time by pulling recent tweets from each followee and merging.
patterns.whenToUse: When a writer has enormous fanout (celebrities) so push would be wasteful.

patterns.name: Hybrid fanout
patterns.what: Push for normal accounts, pull for celebrities, merge at read time.
patterns.whenToUse: Real social graphs with a heavy-tailed follower distribution.
patterns.group: Hybrid Fanout: Push for the Many, Pull for the Few

patterns.name: Async work queue
patterns.what: Offload fanout to a queue so posting returns fast and bursts are absorbed.
patterns.whenToUse: Any expensive side-effect that need not block the user's request.

patterns.name: Cache-aside hydration
patterns.what: Store ids in the timeline, hydrate full tweet bodies from a separate hot cache on read.
patterns.whenToUse: When the same tweet appears in millions of timelines — store it once, reference by id.

steps.title: 1. Naive: Assemble Timeline on Every Read
description: Start with the simplest correct design so the bottleneck is obvious.
On a home-timeline read, look up who the user follows, query the tweet store for each followee's recent tweets, merge them newest-first, and return.
This is correct and trivially consistent — but every read does a fan-in across hundreds of followees against the tweet store.
steps.decisionPrompt: If 300K reads/sec each fan-in across ~200 followees, what load does that put on the tweet store?
whyNow: You must show you understand the read-heavy nature before optimizing — otherwise the interviewer can't tell whether your caching is principled or cargo-culted.

recap.newRisk: Every read is a fan-in of hundreds of queries; the tweet store melts at scale.

traps.trap: Defending read-time assembly as 'good enough' at scale.
traps.why: 300K reads/sec x ~200 followee lookups is ~60M tweet-store queries/sec — impossible on the authoritative store.
traps.instead: Acknowledge it as the baseline and move to precomputation.

steps.title: 2. Fanout-on-Write: Precompute Timelines
description: Flip the cost from read time to write time. When a user posts, look up their followers and push the new tweet id into each follower's cached timeline list.
Reads become a single cache read of a ready-made list of tweet ids — cheap and fast (p99 < 200 ms is now achievable).
Do the fanout asynchronously via a queue so posting returns immediately and traffic bursts are absorbed by workers.
steps.decisionPrompt: Where do you store the precomputed timeline, and what do you store in it — full tweets or just ids?

concepts.definition: Writing a new item into every consumer's precomputed view at production time.
concepts.whyItMatters: Trades cheap reads for expensive writes — the right call when reads dominate.
concepts.example: A 200-follower user posts -> 200 cache list-pushes; reads are then O(1).

whyNow: This is the canonical answer to a read-heavy timeline and sets up the celebrity problem you'll solve next.

recap.after: Reads are a single cached list lookup; posting is fast.
recap.newRisk: A celebrity post means tens of millions of cache writes — fanout storms.

traps.trap: Storing full tweet bodies in every follower's timeline.
traps.why: A celebrity tweet would be copied millions of times, wasting memory and making edits/deletes a nightmare.
traps.instead: Store only tweet ids; hydrate bodies from a shared tweet cache on read.

failureDrills.scenario: A fanout worker crashes mid-job after pushing to half the followers.
failureDrills.expectedBehavior: The job is retried from the queue; pushes are idempotent (LPUSH of an id already present is deduped or capped).
failureDrills.mitigation: At-least-once queue + idempotent timeline writes keyed by tweet id.

steps.title: 3. The Celebrity Problem: Fanout-on-Read
description: Fanout-on-write breaks for accounts with tens of millions of followers: one post triggers tens of millions of cache writes, saturating workers and delaying everyone.
For these few accounts, do the opposite: don't push at all. Pull their recent tweets at read time and merge them into the requesting user's timeline.
Because there are very few celebrities and they're read constantly, their recent tweets stay hot in cache — the pull is cheap.
steps.decisionPrompt: How do you decide an account is a 'celebrity', and what threshold avoids both fanout storms and excessive read-time merging?

concepts.definition: Assembling part of a view at read time by pulling from sources instead of precomputing.
concepts.whyItMatters: Avoids catastrophic write amplification for high-fanout producers.
concepts.example: Skip pushing a 100M-follower account's tweets; pull their last N at read instead.

whyNow: Naming the celebrity problem and solving it asymmetrically is exactly what separates a senior answer from 'just use fanout-on-write'.

recap.before: Every account's tweets were pushed to all followers.
recap.after: Celebrity tweets are pulled at read time; no fanout storm.
recap.newRisk: Reads now have two code paths to reconcile and merge correctly.

traps.why: Then every read fans-in across all followees again — back to the naive bottleneck.
traps.instead: Pull only for the rare high-fanout accounts; push for the vast majority.

steps.title: 4. Hybrid Fanout: Push for the Many, Pull for the Few
description: Combine both: push tweets from normal accounts into follower timelines (cheap reads), and pull recent tweets from the handful of celebrities a user follows at read time.
On read, take the precomputed timeline (push results) and merge in freshly pulled celebrity tweets, then sort newest-first or hand to the ranker.
The threshold is a tuning knob: accounts above ~1M followers (or above some fanout-cost estimate) are flagged is_celebrity and excluded from push.
steps.decisionPrompt: At read time, how do you merge pushed timeline ids with pulled celebrity tweets without double-counting or missing recent posts?
whyNow: This is the design real systems converge on; presenting it as a deliberate reconciliation of the two patterns is the payoff of the whole case.

recap.after: One timeline correctly merges pushed + pulled tweets.
recap.newRisk: Read path complexity; merge logic and the celebrity threshold need monitoring.

deepDives.title: Choosing the celebrity threshold
points: Threshold trades write cost (fanout) against read cost (pull + merge). Too low and reads get heavy; too high and fanout storms return.
Estimate per-account fanout cost = follower_count x post_rate; flip the strategy when that exceeds the read-merge cost.
The flag can change as accounts grow; handle the transition (backfill or accept a brief gap).

traps.trap: Recomputing all timelines when a user crosses the celebrity threshold.
traps.why: That is itself a massive fanout event for a popular account.
traps.instead: Stop pushing going forward and rely on the pull path; optionally backfill lazily.

steps.title: 5. Read Path: Hydrate, Rank, Paginate
description: The timeline cache holds tweet ids; hydrate full tweet bodies from a shared tweet cache (cache-aside, falling back to the tweet store on miss).
Optionally hand candidates to a ranking service for relevance ordering instead of strict reverse-chronological.
Paginate with an opaque cursor (e.g. the last tweet's snowflake id) so infinite scroll is stable as new tweets arrive.
steps.decisionPrompt: Why store tweet ids in the timeline rather than full tweet copies?
whyNow: Shows you separate the timeline (a list of references) from tweet content (shared, hot), which keeps memory bounded and edits/deletes cheap.

concepts.definition: A roughly time-sortable 64-bit id (timestamp + machine + sequence).
concepts.whyItMatters: Lets you sort and paginate timelines by id without a separate timestamp index.
concepts.example: nextCursor = the smallest tweet id returned in this page.

recap.before: Timeline is a list of ids.
recap.after: Reads return fully hydrated, optionally ranked, cursor-paginated tweets.
recap.newRisk: Tweet cache misses on cold tweets; ranker latency budget.

steps.title: 6. Scaling the Social Graph and Stores
description: Shard the social graph by user id; keep two adjacency indexes (followers-of and following-of) so both fanout (needs followers) and read (needs followees) are single-shard lookups.
Shard tweets by author or tweet id; the time-sortable id makes 'recent tweets by author' a range scan.
Shard the timeline cache by user id; each user's timeline lives on one cache node and is independently evictable/rebuildable.
steps.decisionPrompt: Why maintain two separate adjacency indexes instead of deriving one from the other at query time?
whyNow: Sharding choices follow directly from the two access patterns; getting them aligned avoids cross-shard scatter on the hot paths.

recap.after: Graph, tweets, and timelines all shard on the access key.
recap.newRisk: Hot shards for viral tweets / mega-accounts; rebuilding a lost timeline shard.

bottlenecks.issue: A viral tweet makes one tweet-store shard hot for reads.
bottlenecks.mitigation: It's already hydrated from the shared tweet cache, not the store; the cache absorbs the read fanout.

bottlenecks.issue: Losing a timeline cache shard drops those users' precomputed feeds.
bottlenecks.mitigation: Rebuild lazily on next read via the pull path (followees' recent tweets) and resume push.

failureDrills.scenario: A timeline cache node is evicted/restarted and a user's timeline is empty.
failureDrills.expectedBehavior: Reads fall back to assembling from followees (pull) so the user still sees a feed.
failureDrills.mitigation: Treat the timeline cache as rebuildable derived state, never the source of truth.

finalDesign.title: Final Design — Hybrid Fanout Timeline
finalDesign.description: Posts persist to the tweet store and enqueue async fanout; normal accounts push tweet ids into follower timeline caches while celebrities are pulled at read time. Reads merge pushed + pulled ids, hydrate from the shared tweet cache, optionally rank, and paginate by snowflake id. Media is served via CDN.

functional.requirement: Post a tweet visible to followers
functional.how: Write service persists and enqueues fanout; pushed into follower timelines.
Single cached-list read, hydrated and paginated.
Social graph edge changes which timelines a post fans out to.
Write service indexes each tweet; search queries the inverted index.

nonFunctional.how: Precomputed timelines + hot tweet cache make reads O(1) lookups.
Async fanout workers process the queue continuously.
Celebrities are pulled at read time, not pushed.
Timeline cache is rebuildable; reads fall back to pull on cache loss.

say: Confirm it's read-heavy (timeline reads >> posts) and that eventual consistency for fanout is acceptable.
Pin the hard targets: read p99 < 200 ms, posts visible within seconds.
Call out the heavy-tailed follower distribution early — it's the crux.
Start naive (assemble on read) to expose the bottleneck.
Introduce fanout-on-write to make reads cheap.
Then name the celebrity problem and introduce fanout-on-read.
Converge on hybrid fanout and the read-time merge.
Discuss the celebrity threshold as a tuning knob.
Cover sharding of graph, tweets, and timelines aligned to access patterns.
Restate how the design hits each requirement.
Acknowledge tradeoffs: read-path complexity, eventual consistency, threshold tuning.
Mention ranking and media/CDN as natural extensions.

expectations: Gets to fanout-on-write and cheap reads.
May not spot the celebrity problem unprompted.
Names and solves the celebrity problem with hybrid fanout.
Shards stores to match access patterns.
Reasons quantitatively about the celebrity threshold.
Treats timelines as rebuildable derived state with a pull fallback.
Discusses ranking, hot-shard mitigation, and consistency tradeoffs explicitly.

followUps: How would you add relevance ranking without blowing the read latency budget?
How do you handle deletes and edits when a tweet id is referenced in millions of timelines?
How do you keep timelines bounded in memory (cap length, evict cold users)?
How would you support 'see new tweets' real-time updates (push to connected clients)?
How do you backfill a user's timeline when they follow a new account?
